item.title: A Crowded Summer of 2026: How the World Cup Is Reshaping the Sports Calendar
item.body:
## The biggest show on the continent

This summer, North America is the center of the sporting universe. The 2026 FIFA World Cup — the first to span three host nations, the United States, Canada, and Mexico, and the first with an expanded 48-team field — has turned a normally quiet stretch of the calendar into one of the most crowded in memory. For American sports fans, it's a rare moment when soccer isn't competing for attention; it *is* the attention.

## A tournament built bigger

The expansion to 48 teams is the headline structural change, and it cuts both ways. More teams mean more nations get their moment on the sport's grandest stage — debutants and underdogs that would never have qualified under the old format now have a path. Critics counter that a bigger field risks diluting the early rounds with mismatches. The truth will be written on the pitch over the coming weeks, but the ambition is unmistakable: this is the World Cup scaled up to match a continent.

The three-nation format also reshapes the fan experience. Matches sprawl across thousands of miles and multiple time zones, turning the group stage into a genuinely continental event and testing the logistics of moving teams, officials, and supporters across borders.

## The ripple effect on everything else

A tournament this large doesn't sit politely in its own lane. It reshapes the entertainment economy around it:

- **Broadcast and streaming** schedules bend around marquee matches, and the battle for viewers spills into how every other league and event is programmed this summer.
- **Host cities** see a months-long surge of visitors, with knock-on effects for hospitality, transit, and local businesses.
- **Other sports** must decide whether to compete for the spotlight or cede it, scheduling around the matches that will dominate conversation.

## Why it matters beyond the scoreline

For decades, the recurring question has been whether soccer can claim a permanent seat at the table of major American sports. A home tournament of this scale is the most direct test yet — not just of attendance and ratings, but of whether the energy outlasts the final whistle. The matches will crown a champion. The more interesting result may be what the sport's footprint looks like once the stadiums empty and the calendar returns to normal.
